data.title: SRF profit falls 33% to Rs 201.42 crore in September quarter; to invest Rs 1,100 crore on new refrigerant plants
data.description:
SRF Ltd reports Q2 earnings

Chemical firm SRF Ltd on October 22 reported a 33 percent decline in its consolidated net profit to Rs 201.42 crore for the second quarter of this fiscal on higher expenses and announced plans to invest Rs 1,100 crore on new plants to manufacture fourth-generation refrigerants.

Its net profit stood at Rs 300.78 crore in the year-ago period.

Story continues below Advertisement
Remove Ad

The total income rose to Rs 3,457.63 crore during the July-September period of this fiscal from Rs 3,206.48 crore in the year-ago period, the company said in a regulatory filing.

The company's total expenses increased to Rs 3,173.97 crore in the second quarter from Rs 2,791.67 crore a year ago.

SRF Chairman and Managing Director Ashish Bharat Ram said, “While the performance this quarter has been expectedly subdued, I believe the worst is now behind us. We will start seeing an improvement from this quarter onwards with a likelihood of a strong finish to the year".

SRF also informed that its Board of Directors approved "a project for setting up new facilities to manufacture fourth generation refrigerants at Dahej, Gujarat or any other location found suitable by the Company at an expected cost of around Rs 1,100 crore".

The mode of financing will be internal accruals and borrowings, it added.

These facilities aim to meet the future requirements of refrigerants with a significantly lower global warming potential (GWP) and help in lowering carbon footprint.

Story continues below Advertisement
Remove Ad
Story continues below Advertisement
Remove Ad

The plant is expected to be capitalised and commissioned in about 30 months.

Delhi-NCR-based SRF Ltd is into technical textiles, chemicals, and packaging film businesses.

The company has 13 manufacturing plants in India and one each in Thailand, South Africa, and Hungary. It exports to more than 100 countries.
data.author: MoneyControl

data.title: Sebi's insider trading regulations seeks quarterly disclosures from mutual funds: Key details
data.description:
AMCs shall now have to disclose holdings of 'Designated Persons' of AMCs, trustees and their immediate relatives on from November 1, on a quarterly basis.

Effective November 1, regulations governing insider trading shall apply to employees of mutual fund houses as well, bringing MF units under the ambit of the SEBI (Prohibition of Insider Trading) rules.

To effectively implement this amendment, Capital market regulator Sebi has adopted a set of recommendations from the representatives of AMCs, Stock Exchanges, and Depositories:

Story continues below Advertisement
Remove Ad

AMCs shall now have to disclose holdings of 'Designated Persons' of AMCs, trustees and their immediate relatives on from November 1, on a quarterly basis. Now onwards, for every quarter, the AMC has to disclose these holdings within 10 days from the end of the quarter. The regulator has shared a specified format to disclose the MF holdings of the senior employees of the AMC.

The AMC is now required to disclose all transactions of its own mutual funds above Rs 15 lakh in total value, done by the designated employees and their immediate relatives, to the company's Compliance Officer within two working days. Sebi has also shared an annexure format in which these are to be recorded.

The latest circular further adds that employees of a Mutual Fund shall refrain from profiting from buying and selling, or selling and buying of any security with in 30 days from their personal transaction. Instances when this is done shall have to be explained to the Compliance Officer of the AMC, who shall have to report the matter to the board after review.

SEBI had floated a consultation paper in 2022, seeking feedback to effect changes to the (Prohibition of Insider Trading) rules. The approved suggestions will now come into effect in November 1.

This is being updated.
data.author: MoneyControl

data.title: FIIs net sell shares worth Rs 3,979 crore, DIIs net buy shares worth Rs 5,869 crore
data.description:
In the year so far, FIIs have net sold shares worth Rs 2.25 lakh crore, while DIIs have bought Rs 5.01 lakh crore shares.

On October 22, Domestic Institutional Investors (DII) net bought shares worth Rs 5,869 crore. On the other hand, Foreign institutional investors (FIIs) net sold shares worth Rs 3,979 crore, provisional data from NSE showed.

DIIs bought Rs 16,866 crore worth of shares and sold shares worth Rs 10,997 crore. Meanwhile, FIIs purchased shares worth Rs 15,580 crore and offloaded equities worth Rs 19,558 during the trading session.

Story continues below Advertisement
Remove Ad

In the year so far, FIIs have net sold shares worth Rs 2.25 lakh crore, while DIIs have bought shares worth Rs 5.01 lakh crore.

Market view

At close on October 22, the Sensex was down 930.55 points or 1.15 percent at 80,220.72, and the Nifty was down 309.00 points or 1.25 percent at 24,472.10.

Adani Enterprises, M&M, Bharat Electronics, Coal India, Tata Steel were among the top losers on the Nifty, while gainers included ICICI Bank, Nestle India, Infosys.

All the sectoral indices ended in the red

According to Ajit Mishra – SVP, Research, Religare Broking Ltd, the markets continued their downward trend, losing over a percent in an ongoing corrective phase. "Nifty has now corrected by around 7% from its record high, reaching the critical moving average support at the 100 DEMA i.e. 24,485 level. The outlook suggests further downside, particularly in the midcap and smallcap spaces. On the index front, the next major support is around 24,000, with potential resistance between 24,700 and 25,000 in case of a rebound. We recommend adjusting trades accordingly and advise against adding to losing positions," he said.

Story continues below Advertisement
Remove Ad

data.title: Ex-Edelweiss fund manager pays Rs 19.5 lakh to settle allegations of violation of MF Regulations
data.description:
Pending adjudication proceedings, Gupta filed a settlement application, which was placed before the Internal Committee of Sebi on April 18, 2024.

A former fund manager at Edelweiss Asset Management has paid Rs 19.5 lakh to settle allegations of violations of Mutual Fund (MF) Regulations.

A settlement order issued by the Securities and Exchange Board of India (Sebi) on October 22 stated that Abhishek Gupta had been sent a showcause notice asking why inquiry should not be held and penalty not be imposed for an alleged violation. The violation alleged was that Gupta failed to ensure that that the funds of the schemes were invested to achieve the objectives of the scheme.

Story continues below Advertisement
Remove Ad

Thus he had allegedly violated Regulation 25 (6B) (a) of Mutual Funds Regulations and para I (3) read with Annexure A (8) of SEBI circular SEBI/HO/IMD/DF3/CIR/P/2017/114 dated October 6, 2017 and para B(3)(1(a)) of SEBI Circular SEBI/HO/IMD/DF2/CIR/P/2018/92 dated June 05, 2018.

Also read: Sebi moots badges for registered entities on digital platform that facilitates association with regulated players

Pending adjudication proceedings, Gupta filed a settlement application, which was placed before the Internal Committee of Sebi on April 18, 2024.

(This copy will be updated.)
data.author: MoneyControl

data.title: Sebi moots badges for registered entities on digital platform that facilitates association with regulated players
data.description:
The deadline for submission of public comments and suggestions for this consultation paper is November 12, 2024.

The digital platform, through which regulated entities such as brokerages can associate with third parties such as content creators or finfluencers, may need to have a verified label/badge system to identify Sebi-registered entities and their agents.

In a consultation paper released on October 22, the Securities and Exchange Board of India (Sebi) has released the framework that would govern the "specified digital platform" through which regulated entities can associate with third-parties such as finfluencers.

Story continues below Advertisement
Remove Ad

Among the many requirements, the platform may need to have a policy of information-sharing with Sebi and have a policy that allows only content and advertisment from Sebi-registered or recognised entities.

Also read: Sebi asks all regulated entities to terminate contracts with unregistered advisors within three months

The deadline for submission of public comments and suggestions for this consultation paper is November 12, 2024.

The framework has been proposed following the amendments made to Sebi regulations that restrict the association of registered entities with unregistered entities.

According to the consultation paper, these restrictions do not apply if the association is through a digital platform which has the ability to take preventive and curative action to ensure that the association in line with the Sebi regulations.

The paper suggests a framework to govern such a platform.

Story continues below Advertisement
Remove Ad

Some of the requirements the platform may have to meet are as follows:

Usage of a verified label or badge system for SEBI regulated persons and their agents

The platform should have a system to provide verified label or badge to SEBI registered persons to enable users to distinguish between SEBI registered entities and unregistered entities and to allow it to flag for further scrutiny to bring down content/advertisement by unregistered entities.

Policy on collaboration with SEBI:
The platform should have a laid down policy to the satisfaction of SEBI to share the information and data, specific or aggregated, related to securities market on their platform with SEBI when asked for and to act on the information and inputs received from SEBI.

Policy on allowing the entities that provide securities market related content and/or advertisement to operate on the platform:
For the entities providing content and/or advertisement related to securities market, platform should have a policy that it shall allow only -
a) An intermediary/entity registered/recognised by SEBI and their agents to provide content and/or advertisement related to securities market, or
b) A person or an entity or content and/or advertisement which is exempted under any regulations issued by SEBI, or
c) A person who provides/posts/reposts/creates the content and/or advertisement related to securities market exclusively for the purpose of investor education provided the person does not provide any advice or recommendation, directly or indirectly or makes any claim, expressly or impliedly, of return or performance, and does not direct to avail any product or services in respect of or related to security or securities (including related services).

(This copy will be updated.)
data.author: MoneyControl

data.title: Hyundai Motor India disappoints investors on debut, shares decline 7%
data.description:
Hyundai Motor India

Hyundai Motor India, the country's second-largest car maker after Maruti Suzuki, disappointed investors on its debut on the bourses, as shares fell more than 7 percent at close on October 22.

The stock opened at Rs 1,934, down 1.3 percent from its issue price of Rs 1,960 on the National Stock Exchange. It touched an intraday high of Rs 1,970, and low of Rs 1,807, before closing the first session at Rs 1,819.60, down 7.16 percent.

Story continues below Advertisement
Remove Ad

On the BSE, the stock finished at Rs 1,820.4, down 7.12 percent after hitting a day's high of Rs 1,968.8 and low of Rs 1,870.05.

In terms of volume, Hyundai Motor India traded with 2.86 crore equity shares on the NSE, and 15.86 lakh shares on the BSE.

The Indian unit of the South Korean parent Hyundai Motor Company is valued at Rs 1.47 lakh crore now, against Rs 1.59 lakh crore at the issue price.

Also read: India growth story robust, not concerned about near-term pressures in auto industry, says Hyundai's Tarun Garg

Hyundai Motor India raised Rs 27,870 crore through the country's biggest-ever initial share sale last week, which was entirely an offer-for-sale of 14.21 crore equity shares by its parent firm. Hence, the entire issue proceeds (excluding IPO expenses) were received by the promoter.

The price band for the issue was Rs 1,865-1,960 per share.

Story continues below Advertisement
Remove Ad

Post-issue, Hyundai Motor Company holds 82.50 percent stake in its Indian unit and the remaining shareholding of 17.5 percent is owned by public shareholders.

Also read: Hyundai IPO, India’s biggest share sale, is also its most expensive

Domestic institutional investors hold 5.78 percent shares in the carmaker with HDFC Mutual Fund (1.73 percent) and Life Insurance Corporation of India (1.21 percent) being the largest amongst them, while foreign institutional investors hold 7.36 percent stake. The total shareholding by retail investors stands at 3.86 percent in the company.
data.author: MoneyControl

data.title: Sebi asks all regulated entities to terminate contracts with unregistered advisors within three months
data.description:
Regulated entities have to ensure that any person associated with it or its agent does not engage in the activities mentioned in clauses (i) or (ii) without the necessary permission.

Entities regulated by Sebi and the entities' agents have been asked to end all associations with persons who give investment advice/recommendation without being registered with the regulator and with persons who makes claims of returns/performance without permission from the regulator to do so.

A circular issued by the Securities and Exchange Board of India (Sebi)  on October 22, Friday, has asked all regulated entities and their agents to terminate their existing contracts with such persons within three months.

Story continues below Advertisement
Remove Ad

The regulator's Board had given the go ahead for the restricting such associations on June 27. Following that, amendments were made to Sebi regulations these were notified on August 26, 2024.

Also read: SME IPOs: SEBI discussion paper to be out soon to tighten framework

These amended regulations now say that persons regulated by the Board (including recognised stock exchanges, clearing corporations and depositories), and agents of such persons shall not have any direct or indirect association with another person who-

(i) provides advice or any recommendation, directly or indirectly, in respect of or related to a security or securities, unless the person is registered with or otherwise permitted by the Board to provide such advice or recommendation;
or
(ii) makes any claim, of returns or performance expressly or impliedly, in respect of or related to a security or securities, unless the person has been permitted by the Board to make such a claim.

In the latest circular, Sebi has said, "the persons regulated by the Board (including recognised stock exchanges, clearing corporations and depositories), and their agents are advised to terminate their existing contracts, if any, with persons engaged in the activities mentioned in clauses (i) or (ii) of paragraph 2 of this circular, within three months from the date of issuance of this circular.

Regulated entities have to ensure that any person associated with it or its agent does not engage in the activities mentioned in clauses (i) or (ii) without the necessary permission.

Story continues below Advertisement
Remove Ad
data.author: MoneyControl

data.title: Market experts say India and China have different growth approaches and challenges, express optimism
data.description:
Market experts say India and China have different growth approaches and challenges, express optimism

At a time when many in the market are talking about a ‘Sell India, Buy China’ approach adopted by global investors and its effect on the Indian stock markets, a group of experts have said that both India and China have adopted a different strategy to grow and hence can co-exist with an optimistic outlook.

According to Hiren Dasani, Co-head of Global EM Equity and Lead Portfolio Manager, India Equity, Goldman Sachs Asset Management, China has historically relied on manufacturing, while India has focused on consumption and services.

Story continues below Advertisement
Remove Ad

“Today, about 20-25% of global workforces in multinational companies are based in India, signalling a shift from China being the 'factory of the world' to India becoming the 'office of the world,” Dasani said while speaking at the Morningstar Investment Conference.

Dasani explained that China’s export competitiveness has led to increased tariffs and restrictions from the international community, whereas India is integrating into global supply chains and pursuing reforms to enhance corporate earnings.

“If you look at the per capita income, China is at $10,000 today, while India is at about $2,500. Eventually, India will reach that level. India is a democracy, and while progress may be slower, it is more durable. I don’t think there is any commonality between the two countries, other than the fact that both have 1.4 billion people,” he said while adding that India must continue to chart its own goals, regardless of China’s performance.

Similarly, Naren Gorthy of FFSA Investment Managers highlighted how cultural differences influence investment strategies in both countries.

“A good senior management executive in India will be able to immediately break down the return on capital of a company and tell you where the sources of advantage are,” Gorthy explained. He attributes this skill to India's history of capital scarcity, which has necessitated resourcefulness in growth strategies.

Gorthy noted that Indian companies have thrived despite significant challenges, including restrictive government policies. “It is probably generally true that the best Indian companies have done well in spite of the government, not because of it,” he said. This resilience has led to the emergence of world-class companies with strong management teams, a rarity in other parts of the world, he added.

Story continues below Advertisement
Remove Ad

Further, highlighting the distinct entrepreneurial approaches of India and China, the experts said that Indian businesses, often family-owned, tend to operate with a long-term vision, contrasting with China's fast-paced, large-scale ambitions.

While experts acknowledged the unique opportunity presented by the Production Linked Incentive (PLI) scheme for India’s growth, they also recognised some challenges, such as logistics and high labour costs.

Looking ahead, both Dasani and Gorthy expressed confidence in India’s economic prospects. Despite high valuations, they believe the country’s future looks promising, though it will require a careful balance between innovation and opportunities.
data.author: MoneyControl

data.title: Technical View: Nifty breaks 20-week EMA, selling pressure widens to August low if index breaks 24,400
data.description:
Nifty Downtrend

The market fell into a complete bear trap on October 22, as the benchmark Nifty 50 broke several key support levels, indicating that selling pressure may persist for some time. According to experts, if the index decisively breaks the next support at 24,400, the fall may extend to either 24,000 or the August low (around 23,900). However, in the case of a rebound, 24,700 is likely to be an immediate hurdle.

The Nifty 50 opened higher at 24,799 and climbed to 24,882 but could not sustain those gains for long, as bears stepped in. The index finished at 24,472, down 309 points or 1.25 percent, and formed a long bearish candlestick pattern on the daily charts, falling below the 100-day EMA (Exponential Moving Average) for the first time since June 4 this year.

Story continues below Advertisement
Remove Ad

Furthermore, the index dropped decisively below the neckline (around 24,700) of the Head and Shoulders pattern, which coincided with the 20-week SMA, and below the 20-week EMA (24,550), both of which were crucial support levels.

"The sentiment may remain weak as long as the index stays below 24,700, with a 'sell on rise' strategy favoured by market participants," said Rupak De, Senior Technical Analyst at LKP Securities.

According to him, on the lower end, support is placed at 24,400, and if the index falls below this level, it may extend its correction towards 24,000.

On the weekly options data front, the maximum Call open interest was seen at the 25,000 strike, followed by the 25,200 strike, with maximum Call writing at the 24,600 and 24,700 strikes. On the Put side, the 24,000 strike holds the maximum open interest, followed by the 23,500 strike, with maximum writing at the 24,400 and 24,300 strikes.

The above options data suggests a broader trading range between 24,000 and 25,000, with an immediate range between 24,300 and 24,700 for the Nifty 50.

Bank Nifty

Story continues below Advertisement
Remove Ad

The Bank Nifty also fell decisively below the 20-day SMA (which is also the midpoint of the Bollinger Band) as well as below the 50-day EMA, which is a negative sign. The index plunged 706 points, or 1.4 percent, to close at 51,257, forming a long bearish candlestick pattern on the daily charts.

The index corrected by 1,000 points from its intraday high. As long as it remains below the 51,500 zone, weakness could be seen towards 51,000, then 50,750 levels. On the upside, resistance is observed at 51,500 and then 51,750 levels, according to Chandan Taparia, Senior Vice President | Analyst-Derivatives at Motilal Oswal Financial Services.

However, volatility also increased further, climbing above the 14 mark on closing basis, which adds discomfort for bulls. If it sustains above this level, bulls need to be cautious. The VIX index rose by 4.6 percent to a level of 14.4.

Disclaimer: The views and investment tips expressed by investment experts on Moneycontrol.com are their own and not those of the website or its management. Moneycontrol.com advises users to check with certified experts before taking any investment decisions.
